elements.sent-2411-001: Speech-to-Speech Translation (S2ST) models transform speech from one language to another target language with the same linguistic information.
elements.sent-2411-002: S2ST is important for bridging the communication gap among communities and has diverse applications.
elements.sent-2411-003: In recent years, researchers have introduced direct S2ST models, which have the potential to translate speech without relying on intermediate text generation, have better decoding latency, and the ability to preserve paralinguistic and non-linguistic features.
elements.sent-2411-004: However, direct S2ST has yet to achieve quality performance for seamless communication and still lags behind the cascade models in terms of performance, especially in real-world translation.
elements.sent-2411-005: To the best of our knowledge, no comprehensive survey is available on the direct S2ST system, which beginners and advanced researchers can look upon for a quick survey.
elements.sent-2411-006: The present work provides a comprehensive review of direct S2ST models, data and application issues, and performance metrics.
elements.sent-2411-007: We critically analyze the models’ performance over the benchmark datasets and provide research challenges and future directions.
elements.sent-2411-008: The S2ST task is the process of transforming speech in a source language to speech in a target language.
elements.sent-2411-009: It finds applications in live audio/video translation, international conferences and meeting translations, educational video translation, and movie dubbing, to name a few.
elements.sent-2411-010: The rising computational power of computing devices, the expanding bandwidth of communication channels, and the progress in deep learning (DL) and machine learning (ML) techniques have enabled seamless communication.
elements.sent-2411-011: Traditionally, the S2ST problem is solved by the cascade approach in that automatic speech recognition (ASR), machine translation (MT), and text-to-speech synthesis (TTS) models are glued together \cite{Laive_1997, Nakamura2006 .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Another approach to building a cascade S2ST model is by chaining speech-to-text translation (ST) and TTS models.
elements.sent-2411-013: Nonetheless, the cascade models have been the de-facto choice for S2ST for a long time due to the easier availability of the pre-trained component models (ASR, MT, and TTS).
elements.sent-2411-014: However, they are being challenged by direct S2ST models in recent years owing to error propagation, higher training time, and memory cost of cascade models (refer to \S \ref{cascadevse2e for more).}
elements.sent-2411-015: Direct S2ST models translate source speech into target speech without using intermediate text representation.
elements.sent-2411-016: They are being popularised by the possibility of learning paralinguistic and non-linguistic features, including speaking style, emotions, energy, prosody, etc. \cite{Jia2019, Jia2021, Lee2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Languages without a writing system, often called unwritten languages, constitute 40\% of all languages \cite{lee-etal-2022-textless .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Text-based model training is not feasible for these languages.
elements.sent-2411-019: The direct S2ST models can potentially address the challenge posed by unwritten languages \cite{Tjandra_2019_untranscribe, Zhang2021_UWSpeech, lee-etal-2022-textless .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Direct models follow an end-to-end (E2E) training approach, reducing error propagation and offering lower latency than cascade models \cite{Jia2019, Jia2021, Lee2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-021: However, despite their advantages, these models encounter notable challenges such as: (a) getting sufficient parallel speech corpus in two different languages is extremely hard, thus hampering model development, (b) training with the speech of unwritten languages and their evaluation, (c) the potential threat of voice cloning, (d) absence of metrics directly taking generated and reference speech as input and returning a quality score, and (e) segmentation issue especially in simultaneous S2ST, and so on.
elements.sent-2411-022: Further advancements are still required for S2ST systems to attain the level of quality necessary for hassle-free communication.
elements.sent-2411-023: To provide an overview of research done in the S2ST field and a bucket of open problems, the present work comprehensively reviews the direct S2ST literature, whose broad taxonomy is shown in Fig. \ref{taxonomy .}
elements.sent-2411-024: Overall, the manuscript is organized as follows: \S\ref{Task_definition defines the S2ST task ; \S\ref{cascadevse2e presents the cascade vs E2E models; \S\ref{datapaucity discusses the data scarcity.}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: The performance metrics are presented in \S\ref{metrics while \S\ref{repr elucidates the segmentation and representation issues.}}
elements.sent-2411-026: Models and training strategies are discussed in \S\ref{sec:direct_s2st and \S\ref{trainstr , respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-027: Application issues are discussed in \S\ref{application , experiments in \S\ref{experiment , challenges in \S\ref{challenges , and finally concluded in \S\ref{challenges .}}}}

elements.sent-2411-028: Given a parallel speech corpus, denoted as $\mathcal{D =\{(x_i,y_i)\}_{i=1 ^n$.}}
elements.sent-2411-029: In this context, $x=\{f^s_1,\ldots, f^s_k\}$ and $y=\{f^t_1,\ldots, f^t_l\}$ represents the source and target speech utterances, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-030: Here, $f^s$ and $f^t$ refer to the source and target speech frames, and $k$ and $l$ represent utterance length in frames, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-031: The objective of direct S2ST models is to maximize the conditional probability $\mathcal{P (y|x;\theta)$ as in \eqref{condobj or minimize the negative log-likelihood loss \eqref{nll . \begin{equation}\label{condobj} \mathcal{P}(y|x;\theta)=\overset{k}{\underset{T=1}{\prod}}\mathcal{P}(f^t_T|f^t_{<T},x;\theta)\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{nll} \mathcal{L}_{(x,y)\in D}=-\overset{n}{\underset{i=1}{\sum}} \log \mathcal{P}(y_i|x_i;\theta)\end{equation} where $\mathcal{L _{(x,y)\in D}$ is the cumulative loss on the dataset $D$ and $\theta$ is a model parameter.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: Note that the problem formulation given in \eqref{condobj is for Autoregressive (AR) models \footnote{Non-autoregressive (NAR) models are an alternative modeling approach that have been proposed in the past few years.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Only a sparse number of works exist in the literature on S2ST where they use NAR in decoders of encoder-decoder frameworks.
elements.sent-2411-034: We discuss NAR briefly in \S \ref{decoderarch On the other hand, cascade models have access to source transcripts denoted as a sequence of tokens (words) $w^s= \{w^s_1, \ldots, w^s_p\}$, and the target transcript as a sequence of tokens, represented as $w^t = \{w^t_1, \ldots, w^t_q\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-035: In the 3-stage cascade model, the optimization objectives are as follows: \begin{equation} \mathcal{L}_{asr}=-\overset{n}{\underset{i=1}{\sum}} \log \mathcal{P}(w^s_i|x_i;\theta^{asr}) \label{eq:asr_loss}\end{equation} \begin{equation} \mathcal{L}_{mt}=-\overset{n}{\underset{i=1}{\sum}} \log \mathcal{P}(w^t_i|w^s_i;\theta^{mt}) \label{eq:mt_loss}\end{equation} \begin{equation} \mathcal{L}_{tts}=-\overset{n}{\underset{i=1}{\sum}} \log \mathcal{P}(f^t_i|w^t_i;\theta^{tts}) \label{eq:tts_loss}\end{equation} \begin{equation} \mathcal{L}_{st}=-\overset{n}{\underset{i=1}{\sum}} \log \mathcal{P}(w^t_i|x_i;\theta^{st}) \label{eq:st_loss}\end{equation} A 3-stage cascade model is built by independently minimizing the losses in \eqref{eq:asr_loss , \eqref{eq:mt_loss , and \eqref{eq:tts_loss .}}}
elements.sent-2411-036: A direct E2E ST model \cite{Berard2018_Audiobook, Kano2020 optimizes the loss in \eqref{eq:st_loss (may also use losses in \eqref{eq:asr_loss , \eqref{eq:mt_loss in a multitask setup).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: This ST model is combined with the TTS model to form a 2-stage cascade S2ST model.
elements.sent-2411-038: The traditional S2ST systems follow a cascade architecture \cite{Laive_1997, Ney_1999_ST, Nakamura2006, Wahlster2000_Verbmobli .}
elements.sent-2411-039: They are designed either by chaining ASR, MT, and TTS or ST followed by TTS as illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:S2ST_Cascade_Vs_Direct (i) and (ii) respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Either way, the cascade system relies on intermediate text.
elements.sent-2411-041: As such, they face several issues in modeling S2ST effectively.
elements.sent-2411-042: Firstly, they face challenges when dealing with low-resource languages lacking annotated corpora or unwritten languages \cite{Chen2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-043: Secondly, paralinguistic features such as prosody, intonation, emotions, etc. are lost when representing speech via intermediate text, quintessential for building a real-world S2ST system.
elements.sent-2411-044: Thirdly, error propagation from one module to another \cite{Jia2019 , higher training time \cite{Huang2022_TranSpeech , and memory footprint (due to 3 models vs 1 in direct S2ST) prohibit their application to low-powered devices.}}
elements.sent-2411-045: The above issues with cascade systems catapulted the development of direct S2ST systems bypassing the intermediate text generation, reducing training time and memory cost.
elements.sent-2411-046: There has been a lot of work developing direct S2ST models \cite{Jia2019, Jia2021, jia-etal-2022-cvss, Huang2022_TranSpeech, diwan2024textless , etc. Therefore, it is imperative to compare cascade and direct models on quantitative and qualitative metrics (discussed in \S \ref{metrics ).}}
elements.sent-2411-047: Our literature survey reveals that there was a performance gap between cascade and direct models (both in terms of BLEU and mean opinion score (MOS)) \cite{Jia2019, Jia2021, lee-etal-2022-textless, zhu-etal-2023-diffs2ut, Huang2022_TranSpeech which is now almost closed \cite{Chen2022, peng2024mslms2st .}}
elements.sent-2411-048: These studies, however, are done on limited language pairs and may not generalize.
elements.sent-2411-049: Therefore, it remains to see an exhaustive comparison over multiple and distant language pairs involving large-scale datasets to truly establish the claim that the performance gap is indeed closed.
elements.sent-2411-050: Parallel speech corpora are crucial for training direct S2ST models, and their scarcity can significantly affect their training, performance, and robustness.
elements.sent-2411-051: Creating parallel speech corpora requires substantial resource investment \cite{Chen2023_DataAugment_survey , resulting in limited available datasets, as shown in Table \ref{Dataset_Stats in the appendix.}}
elements.sent-2411-052: While this scarcity of supervised S2ST datasets prevents the application of direct S2ST systems to low-resource languages, techniques like data augmentation, pre-training, self-training, back-translation, knowledge distillation, and speech mining are employed to address data scarcity and enable S2ST for low-resource languages.
elements.sent-2411-053: These methods are further elaborated in the following sections.
elements.sent-2411-054: Data augmentation (DA) refers to techniques that boost the number of data points to enhance diversity without requiring the explicit collection of real supervised data points \cite{Feng2021_DataAug_survey .}
elements.sent-2411-055: The DA techniques suitable for various NLP tasks may not be directly applicable to S2ST since S2ST involves parallel speech, posing a challenge for augmenting existing data.
elements.sent-2411-056: Speech data can be augmented in various ways.
elements.sent-2411-057: For example, by adding noise, speed and pitch perturbation, time and frequency masking, etc. In \cite{Jia2021 , data augmentation technique ConcatAug is employed to preserve the speaker's voice on their turn.}
elements.sent-2411-058: In particular, training data is enhanced by randomly selecting pairs of training examples and concatenating their source speech, target speech, and target phoneme sequences to create new training examples.
elements.sent-2411-059: These new examples incorporate the voices of two different speakers in both the source and target speech, allowing the model to learn from instances that include speaker turns.
elements.sent-2411-060: Many studies utilize synthesized speech from the target text in ST data \cite{Jia2019, Jia2021, Lee2022, nachmani_2023_translatotron3 , while others make use of MT and TTS models to label ASR corpora through pseudo-labeling \cite{Dong2022_Psudo_Labeled, Jia2022_Leveraging, Popuri2022_Enahancing_SelfSupervised,huang-etal-2023-xiaomi .}}
elements.sent-2411-061: Pre-training \cite{Popuri2022_Enahancing_SelfSupervised, Wei_2013_joint_pre-train_s2st aims to improve the performance of the task in one domain with the learned knowledge from the tasks in another domain \cite{Pan2010_TransferLearning, Wang2022_Pre-Training_Application .}}
elements.sent-2411-062: Models learn salient features from an extensive training corpus during pre-training.
elements.sent-2411-063: Subsequently, the pre-trained model is applied to another (target) task, employing smaller datasets for fine-tuning~\cite{Van_dem_ordo_RepresentationLearning,tang-etal-2022-unified .}
elements.sent-2411-064: In recent works, self-supervised strategies are used for pre-training, to leverage the availability of large unlabeled datasets.
elements.sent-2411-065: Several pre-trained DL models, including BERT \cite{Devlin2019_BERT , BART \cite{lewis-etal-2020-BART (using unit mBART version), Wav2Vec 2.0 \cite{baevski2020_wav2vec2.0 , HuBERT \cite{Hsu_2021_HuBERT , w2v-BERT \cite{Chung2021_w2V-BERT , and VQ-VAE \cite{Van_Oord_2017_VQ-VAE , which are trained on extensive unlabeled data, serve as the foundation for various NLP and speech tasks.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-066: The pre-trained models are used as encoders/decoders in direct S2ST models \cite{bansal2018pre, tsiamas-etal-2022-pretrained, Lee2022_Representation_Learning_for_Speech (refer to \S \ref{sec:direct_s2st ).}}
elements.sent-2411-067: Self-training and Back-translation (BT) algorithms leverage monolingual data to train models requiring supervised data but lacking a supervised parallel corpus.
elements.sent-2411-068: These algorithms use the model's own confident predictions for monolingual data, which are initially trained on available parallel data \cite{edunov-etal-2018-understanding_BT, yang2022survey_semi_super .}
elements.sent-2411-069: Self-training utilizes source monolingual data, while BT utilizes target monolingual data to generate augmented data.
elements.sent-2411-070: Let us consider a utterance-wise parallel speech corpus denoted as $\mathcal{D =\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1 ^n$, with $(x_i,y_i)$ representing the source and target utterance, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-071: Additionally, we have source monolingual corpus $\mathcal{M _{src = \{u_j\}_{j = 1 ^m$, and target monolingual corpus $\mathcal{M _{tgt = \{v_k\}_{k = 1 ^q$, where \(m,q \gg n\).}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-072: The forward translation model $f_{x\rightarrow y}$ is optimized for the parameter \(\theta_{x\rightarrow y = \text{argmin _\theta \sum_{(x_i, y_i) \in \mathcal{D \mathcal{L (f^\theta_{x\rightarrow y (x_i), y_i)\), where $\mathcal{L}$ is the loss function.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-073: Similarly, another model in backward direction, $f_{y\rightarrow x}$, is optimized for the parameter \(\theta_{y\rightarrow x = \text{argmin _\theta \sum_{(x_i, y_i) \in \mathcal{D \mathcal{L (f^\theta_{y\rightarrow x (y_i), x_i)\).}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-074: Using the already trained model \(f_{x \rightarrow y}\) and \(\mathcal{M _{src}\), pseudo-labeled utterances \(\hat{y}\) are generated to create a new auxiliary parallel corpus $\mathcal{A ^s = \{(u_j, \hat{y _j)\}_{j=1 ^{m'}$ by selecting confident predictions.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-075: In self-training, as shown in Figure \ref{fig:BT_KD_ST (a), the model \(f_{x \rightarrow y}\) is retrained on the augmented data \(\mathcal{D \cup \mathcal{A ^s\) \cite{He2020_revisiting_SelfTraining .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-076: Similarly, an auxiliary parallel corpus \(\mathcal{A ^t = \{(\hat{x _k, v_k)\}_{k=1 ^{q' \) is generated using the backward-trained model \(f_{y \rightarrow x}\).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-077: When employing back-translation (BT) the model \(f_{x \rightarrow y}\) is trained in the forward direction on the newly augmented data \(\mathcal{D \cup \mathcal{A ^t\) as depicted in Figure \ref{fig:BT_KD_ST (b) \cite{sennrich-etal-2016-improving .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: Various studies utilize a denoising version of BT, where noise is added to the input \cite{fu_2023_improving_BackTranslation .}
elements.sent-2411-079: Several studies demonstrate that self-training \cite{Pino2020_ST_4ST and BT \cite{Pino2020_ST_4ST are highly beneficial, particularly for low-resource languages compared to high-resource ones.}}
elements.sent-2411-080: These algorithms also benefit S2ST directly or indirectly, with the indirect method proving more effective \cite{Popuri2022_Enahancing_SelfSupervised,nachmani_2023_translatotron3 .}
elements.sent-2411-081: Knowledge Distillation (KD) transfers learned knowledge from a large ensemble model to a smaller model as shown in Figure \ref{fig:BT_KD_ST (c) \cite{Hinton2015_KnowledgeDistilation, Treviso2023_NLP_Methods_Survey .}}
elements.sent-2411-082: The KD process is based on the teacher-student learning paradigm: the larger model serves as the teacher, while the smaller model acts as the student.
elements.sent-2411-083: KD proves to be valuable for tasks such as ASR \cite{Hinton2015_KnowledgeDistilation , ST \cite{Inaguma2021, Liu2019d_KnowledgeDistillation , and S2ST \cite{Huang2022_TranSpeech in scenarios with limited resources.}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: In particular, AV-TranSpeech \citet{Huang2022_TranSpeech applies cross-modal distillation from audio-only pre-trained S2ST \cite{Popuri2022_Enahancing_SelfSupervised to AV-TranSpeech.}}
elements.sent-2411-085: Doing so initializes the audio encoder and unit decoder and alleviates the low-resource problem of audio-visual data.
elements.sent-2411-086: Nonetheless, this approach remains relatively under-explored in the context of direct S2ST models.
elements.sent-2411-087: Speech mining is an extension of bitext mining \cite{resnik-1998-parallel , designed to discover parallel speech corpora from monolingual speech corpora.}
elements.sent-2411-088: Bitext refers to text-to-text parallel data where the source and target sentences are in different languages.
elements.sent-2411-089: Bitext mining has been done in a supervised and unsupervised way (no cross-lingual resources like parallel text or bilingual lexicons are used) \cite{keung-etal-2020-unsupervised .}
elements.sent-2411-090: Multilingual fixed-length sentence embedding \cite{heffernan-etal-2022-bitext using KD, contrastive learning \cite{tan2022bitext are used for mining bitext in low-resource settings.}}
elements.sent-2411-091: Sentence embedding serves to cluster similar sentences closely together within the latent space \cite{schwenk-2018-filtering,artetxe-schwenk-2019-massively .}
elements.sent-2411-092: Similarly, for speech mining, multilingual fixed-length speech embedding is employed to represent the variable-length speech utterances.
elements.sent-2411-093: Speech mining has been used by a few works such as \citet{Duquenne_2021_Speech_Mining where they utilize a teacher-student approach to train the multilingual speech embedding, which produces speech representations that are compatible with the output of text embedding layer.}
elements.sent-2411-094: Offline S2ST systems are evaluated using Various text-based metrics such as BLEU \cite{papineni-etal-2002-bleu , ScareBLEU \cite{Post2018_scareBLEU , BLEURT \cite{Sellam2020_BLEURT , COMET \cite{Rei2020_COMET, Rei2022_COMET-22 , and chrF \cite{Popovic_2015_chrF are employed to measure the quality of translated speech.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-095: These metrics are used after transcribing the speech through ASR due to the absence of direct evaluation metrics.
elements.sent-2411-096: These metrics depend on the performance of ASR models, and they also pose challenges for low-resource and unwritten languages.
elements.sent-2411-097: This is primarily due to either the unavailability of ASR systems or the existence of poor-quality models \cite{Salesky2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-098: Recently, \citet{Chen2023_BLASER introduced a metric called BLASER\footnote{Balanced Accuracy for Speech Evaluation by Recognition , which is a text-free S2ST quality metric.}}
elements.sent-2411-099: BLASER takes source, reference, and generated speeches, embeds them to a common representation space via a shared encoder, and finally computes a score via a neural regressor.
elements.sent-2411-100: Formally, unsupervised BLASER score is given below. \begin{equation}\text{BLASER}_U = \frac{cos(h_{src}, h_{mt})+cos(h_{mt},h_{ref})}{2}\end{equation} where cos(·, ·) is the cosine similarity function, $h_{src , h_{mt}$, and $h_{ref}$ are the source, generated, and reference speech representations, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-101: There is also a supervised BLASER score proposed \cite{Chen2023_BLASER .}
elements.sent-2411-102: Naturalness measures how closely synthesized speech resembles natural speech.
elements.sent-2411-103: This is measured by a human-based subjective metric known as MOS (Mean Opinion Score).
elements.sent-2411-104: It is a numerical measure that represents the average subjective rating given by human listeners to the quality of a speech signal.
elements.sent-2411-105: MOS provides a simple and intuitive measure of speech quality that correlates well with human perception.
elements.sent-2411-106: Though MOS is widely used for speech quality, it has a few limitations such as subjective evaluation, bias, limited sample size, time, and cost.
elements.sent-2411-107: Therefore, it is recommended also to report some objective metrics along with MOS, especially those that capture the perception of speech quality.
elements.sent-2411-108: For example, the Perceptual Evaluation of Speech Quality (PESQ) \cite{rix2001perceptual , its extension Perceptual Objective Listening Quality Analysis (POLQA) \cite{beerends2013perceptual , Short-Time Objective Intelligibility (STOI) \cite{taal2011algorithm are a few objective algorithms designed to predict the perceived quality of speech as heard by human listeners.}}}
elements.sent-2411-109: Voice-preservation measures the extent to which predicted speech is similar to a particular speaker's voice.
elements.sent-2411-110: It can be calculated using metrics for evaluating voice cloning.
elements.sent-2411-111: Measuring voice cloning involves a combination of subjective and objective methods to evaluate various aspects of the cloned voice, such as its similarity to the original voice (via speaker embedding similarity as done in \cite{Dong2023_PolyVoice ), naturalness, intelligibility, and acoustic properties.}
elements.sent-2411-112: Simultaneous S2ST (Simul-S2ST) systems are evaluated using quality-based metrics for offline S2ST models along with latency metrics.
elements.sent-2411-113: Calculating the latency of the offline S2ST is straightforward: It is equal to the time elapsed in starting to produce the output from the decoder.
elements.sent-2411-114: For Simul-S2ST, calculating the average latency poses a significant challenge since it sees only the partial input.
elements.sent-2411-115: Due to the absence of such metrics, simultaneous text-to-text (Simul-T2T) latency metrics such as AP (average proportion) \cite{cho2016neural_AP and AL (Average Lagging) \cite{ma-etal-2019-stacl_AL are used as proxy.}}
elements.sent-2411-116: AL is further adapted for the Simul-S2T task \cite{Ren2020_SimulSpeech , which is also used for the Simul-S2ST task with the help of ASR due to the non-availability of direct metrics.}
elements.sent-2411-117: Discussion: Current practice in S2ST works is that authors report only text-based quality metrics ignoring objective and subjective metrics as described in this section.
elements.sent-2411-118: It is warranted that S2ST models are evaluated holistically using objective and subjective metrics as well.
elements.sent-2411-119: Further, the development of more effective textless S2ST quality metrics is recommended.
elements.sent-2411-120: S2ST models essentially take speech and optionally take text as inputs.
elements.sent-2411-121: Before we can train E2E S2ST models, we need to segment the speech and text followed by how to learn their representations.
elements.sent-2411-122: Handling long speech and text sequences is a challenging task \cite{kim2017joint, Tsiamas2022SHASAO .}
elements.sent-2411-123: The following section discusses segmentation and representation learning related to speech and text.
elements.sent-2411-124: Breaking down text into segments is a simpler task--it involves splitting based on robust punctuation marks used by current MT models.
elements.sent-2411-125: E2E S2ST models necessitate intricate speech segmentation, primarily because of the significant role played by the out-of-order word relationships between input and output, alongside the absence of linguistic features.
elements.sent-2411-126: Traditionally, manual segmentation has been the norm for speech processing.
elements.sent-2411-127: However, due to its labor-intensive nature, there is a growing need for segmentation learning.
elements.sent-2411-128: Speech segmentation typically relies on either fixed-length splits which split the speech at fixed lengths, sometimes randomly \cite{Huang2022_TranSpeech or pause which splits the speech on Voice Activity Detection (VAD), as outlined by \citet{Sohn1999ASM .}}
elements.sent-2411-129: A third method, the hybrid approach, integrates both length and linguistic cues for segmentation, as discussed by \cite{Potapczyk2020SRPOLsSF, Gaido2021BeyondVA, Tsiamas2022SHASAO .}
elements.sent-2411-130: Notably, the hybrid approach demonstrates superior performance compared to length and pause-based methods \citep{Gaido2021BeyondVA .}
elements.sent-2411-131: However, there is still a gap in the hybrid and manual approaches to segmentation, and future work may consider paying attention to this.
elements.sent-2411-132: Representation learning is an important issue in S2ST because speech in two different languages are two different modalities that may reside in different representation spaces.
elements.sent-2411-133: Hence, we not only need better representation learning methods for speech and text but also their joint representation learning.
elements.sent-2411-134: Existing works leveraging text data for S2ST modeling utilize LSTM \cite{Kano2021_Transformer , Transformers \cite{Lee2022 , etc. for representation learning.}}
elements.sent-2411-135: As the current trend is towards building textless models, more recent works focus on learning efficient speech representations.
elements.sent-2411-136: Among them, the popular choices are raw waveform, spectrogram-based, unsupervised ASR, and discrete-units based.
elements.sent-2411-137: The raw waveform is the use of raw speech signal directly fed to the Sequence-to-Sequence (Seq2Seq) model and has been utilized by \cite{wang2023speechtospeech, KimLCL24 , inter alia.}
elements.sent-2411-138: Mel-Filter Cepstral Coefficient (MFCC) feature, a spectrogram-based method, has been one of the most used speech representation methods \cite{Jia2019, jia2019leveraging_S2T, Tjandra_2019_untranscribe, Kano2021_Transformer, Lee2022, Huang2022_TranSpeech, Chen2023 , etc. where 80-dimension mel-spectrogram is computed.}
elements.sent-2411-139: Obtaining a substantial volume of labeled speech data for supervised feature representation learning poses significant challenges.
elements.sent-2411-140: Consequently, recent studies have turned to leveraging speech features acquired through unsupervised and self-supervised methods.
elements.sent-2411-141: These approaches involve mapping continuous speech signals into discrete units-- akin to words and sub-words in the text domain.
elements.sent-2411-142: Such representations enable the integration of NLP tools into the speech domain.
elements.sent-2411-143: Among them, the popular choices are Wav2Vec \citep{schneider2019wav2vec and its variants such as w2v-BERT \citep{Chung2021_w2V-BERT, Jia2022_Leveraging and Wav2Vec 2.0 \citep{baevski2020_wav2vec2.0, Chen2022, song_2023_styles2st and Hidden-Units BERT (HuBERT) \citep{hsu2021hubert .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-144: What makes these representation methods take over the MFCC is that they can extract semantic-units.

elements.sent-2411-148: Direct S2ST models can be classified broadly into three main categories: offline, simultaneous (Simul), and LLM-based S2ST\footnote{LLM-based S2ST is a very recent approach to S2ST modeling and is given special treatment due to their superior performance.. .}
elements.sent-2411-149: Offline models, as the name suggests, start decoding after having seen the entire utterance, whereas simultaneous (aka streaming models) models can start decoding only with partial utterances.
elements.sent-2411-150: As such, Simul-S2ST is amenable to real-time translation, dubbing, etc. In this section, we will delve deeper into these three category models.
elements.sent-2411-151: In particular, we will discuss the architectural level changes made in vanilla Seq2Seq to devise the offline/Simul/LLM-S2ST models.
elements.sent-2411-152: As mentioned previously, offline direct S2ST models start decoding after having seen the entire utterance.
elements.sent-2411-153: The typical architecture of such models is shown in Fig. \ref{fig:GenericDirectS2ST which is based on Seq2Seq with attention (not shown in Fig.).}

elements.sent-2411-155: Dashed arrow components are optional and used in a few existing works.
elements.sent-2411-156: This section will discuss various design choices used by existing works for translation model component as well as vocoder.
elements.sent-2411-157: Style adapters and duration predictors will also be explained as they play an important role in designing offline direct S2ST models.

elements.sent-2411-159: Others integrate both into a single and directly feed raw speech into the translation model's encoder \cite{Popuri2022_Enahancing_SelfSupervised, Li2023_TextLess_Direct .}
elements.sent-2411-160: For example, Translatotron 1 \cite{Jia2019 uses a spectrogram as input fed to the LSTM encoder.}

elements.sent-2411-162: Essentially, recent direct S2ST models rely on transformers and their variants for encoder \cite{lee-etal-2022-textless, Li2023_TextLess_Direct .}

elements.sent-2411-168: Decoders in the existing works have been designed either in a Autoregressive (AR) or Non-autoregressive (NAR) way, as explained below.
elements.sent-2411-169: Further, they could be single-pass or double-pass decoders.
elements.sent-2411-170: Double-pass decoders employ a linguistic and an acoustic decoder, contrary to single-pass decoders with only an acoustic decoder.
elements.sent-2411-171: Due to multi-level speech processing and the ability to solve complex multimodal distribution\footnote{S2ST is considered a multimodal problem due to (a) linguistic diversity during translation, and (b) diverse acoustic conditions, e.g., pitch, duration, energy, etc. , double-pass decoders are shown to outperform single-pass decoders \cite{Jia2021, inaguma_2023_unity .}}
elements.sent-2411-172: In AR decoding, output tokens are predicted in a causal manner, i.e., current output depends only on the previously generated tokens.
elements.sent-2411-173: LSTMs and transformer-based decoders are preferred choices for autoregressive decoding.
elements.sent-2411-174: For example, unit mBART is used as a unit decoder in \cite{Popuri2022_Enahancing_SelfSupervised, diwan2024textless whereas Translatotron 1 and 2 use LSTMs.}

elements.sent-2411-209: Simul-S2ST is an important problem; however, simultaneously enhancing translation quality while reducing latency presents a formidable challenge.
elements.sent-2411-210: The Simul-S2ST problem faces several issues in practical implementation; {\bf reordering, acoustic ambiguity, variable speech rate, and long inputs being prominent among them.}

elements.sent-2411-233: Pre-training, self-supervised, unsupervised, and weakly supervised training approaches are primarily used to solve data scarcity as mentioned in \S \ref{datapaucity .}

elements.subsection-2411-007: Training w/o External Data aka Textless Training
elements.sent-2411-249: Compared to high-resource written languages, low-resource unwritten languages lack transcripts.
elements.sent-2411-250: For these languages, recent efforts in direct S2ST modeling propose textless training.
elements.sent-2411-251: One approach is to train the model on speech spectrograms, but this method struggles to learn generalized patterns without text.
elements.sent-2411-252: Alternatively, textless training can use self-supervised (e.g., HuBERT) or unsupervised (e.g., VQ-VAE) discrete unit speech encoders.
elements.sent-2411-253: This encoder converts continuous speech into discrete tokens (similar to text), enabling the application of textual NLP tools to speech.
elements.sent-2411-254: For example, The textless model in \cite{Zhang2021_UWSpeech comprises three modules: converter, translator, and inverter.}
elements.sent-2411-255: The converter transforms target speech into discrete units, the translator translates source speech into target discrete units, and the inverter inverts the predicted discrete units back into a speech waveform.
elements.sent-2411-256: Target discrete units enable the model to use the cross-entropy loss for model optimization.
elements.sent-2411-257: This architecture is beneficial for untranscribed languages \cite{Tjandra_2019_untranscribe, Zhang2021_UWSpeech,lee-etal-2022-textless, Huang2022_TranSpeech or speech datasets without labelled transcripts \cite{Huang2022_TranSpeech, Lee2022 .}}
elements.sent-2411-258: The process of acquiring phonetic knowledge from languages that share syntactic similarities and have a written form might aid in learning representations for unwritten languages \cite{yallop2007_phonetics_phonology, Kuhl2008_phonetics_learning .}
elements.sent-2411-259: Nevertheless, the extent to which this assistance proves effective depends on the degree of similarity between the languages.
elements.sent-2411-260: Hence, leveraging the benefits of related languages with writing systems, XL-VAE \cite{Zhang2021_UWSpeech is trained in a supervised manner by using phonemes from the related language as targets.}
elements.sent-2411-261: Offline direct S2ST models find applications in the dubbing of movies and lectures.
elements.sent-2411-262: When deploying direct S2ST models for such applications, several things to be kept in mind.
elements.sent-2411-263: Firstly, getting a clean speech from a real-world environment is a challenging task.
elements.sent-2411-264: Cross-talk, noise, and background music removal must be done.
elements.sent-2411-265: Secondly, the challenge lies in handling various accents while translating, and voice preservation should be taken care of.
elements.sent-2411-266: The third important requirement of dubbing is {\bf isochrony , i.e. dubbed speech has to closely match the timing of speech and pauses of the original audio.}
elements.sent-2411-267: Inserting pauses, handling verbosity, and prosodic alignment are a few techniques to achieve isochrony.
elements.sent-2411-268: On the other hand, Simul-S2ST is more practical for real-world dubbing.
elements.sent-2411-269: Contrary to offline S2ST, these models require latency-quality handled in a better way along with isochrony.

elements.sent-2411-291: Our literature review reveals a gap in research regarding the S2ST model applied to code-mix data.

elements.subsection-2411-010: Discrepancy between Automatic and Human Evaluation
elements.sent-2411-294: Current S2ST systems are evaluated by conducting ASR on the generated waveform and comparing it to the ground truth target text.
elements.sent-2411-295: Errors in ASR can impede evaluation via BLEU.
elements.sent-2411-296: As emphasized in \citep{marie-etal-2021-scientific , the BLEU score is reported by more than 99\% of MT papers without considering statistical significance testing or human evaluation.}
elements.sent-2411-297: Our examination of S2ST papers indicates a similar trend.
elements.sent-2411-298: The development of metrics that directly compare and evaluate source and target speech utterances without resorting to textual analysis remains an open challenge.
elements.sent-2411-299: In real-world scenarios, audio or video often feature multiple speakers in a noisy environment, each potentially with their accent, dialect, pitch, and tone.
elements.sent-2411-300: Conducting speech separation beforehand could prove beneficial before inputting the data into the S2ST.
elements.sent-2411-301: Similarly, factors such as ambient noise, background music, cross-talk, and non-verbal sounds can pose challenges for S2ST model training.
elements.sent-2411-302: Distinguishing between meaningful speech and ambient noise presents a non-trivial task for the model.
elements.sent-2411-303: The recent surge in interest in multilingual S2ST is driven by its significance in real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-304: For instance, a single speech may need to be delivered to multilingual communities, such as during a conference attended by a diverse audience.
elements.sent-2411-305: Multilingual S2ST can encompass various scenarios, including one-to-many, many-to-one, and many-to-many language translations.
elements.sent-2411-306: However, our literature review indicates a scarcity of research in this area.
elements.sent-2411-307: Moreover, there is an opportunity to explore simultaneous multilingual S2ST\footnote{In May 2024, Microsoft launched such a service in their Azure product.}
elements.sent-2411-308: Most current research efforts have concentrated on constructing S2ST models and datasets for languages with ample resources.
elements.sent-2411-309: However, given that the effectiveness of S2ST models hinges on parallel speech-to-speech corpora, there is a need for greater emphasis on developing datasets for low-resource languages.
elements.sent-2411-310: Thus, there is merit in constructing models that can transfer learning from language pairs with abundant resources to those with limited resources.
elements.sent-2411-311: The true challenge for direct models lies in attaining both lower latency and higher translation quality to fulfill real-time usage requirements.
elements.sent-2411-312: Simultaneously preserving the authenticity of the voice presents a challenge, as it is essential to guard against the misuse of voice cloning.
elements.sent-2411-313: A hybrid model design combining discrete units and spectrograms can acquire linguistic and para-linguistic features, thereby enhancing translation quality, naturalness, and voice preservation.
elements.sent-2411-314: Many direct models primarily rely on autoregressive models as their sub-modules, in which the current output depends on previous inputs, resulting in a high degree of input dependency.
elements.sent-2411-315: Decreasing the number of modules or layers is essential to reduce the average latency of direct S2ST models.
elements.sent-2411-316: Moreover, shifting the focus from autoregressive models to non-autoregressive models is advisable.
elements.sent-2411-317: This shift in focus is crucial for enabling real-time usage scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-318: We present a comprehensive survey of direct S2ST models.
elements.sent-2411-319: In particular, we discussed the comparison of E2E and cascade models, data scarcity, representation, and segmentation issues in S2ST modeling.
elements.sent-2411-320: A bird-eye view of architectures of offline, simultaneous, and LLM-based S2ST models is discussed in detail.
elements.sent-2411-321: We also offer training strategies and application issues that may be beneficial to industry practitioners.
elements.sent-2411-322: Finally, we enumerate a list of open problems and challenges that remain to be solved.
elements.sent-2411-323: Thus, we hope that the review will be a torch-bearer for someone starting their work in the S2ST domain.. \bibliographystyle{acl_natbib .}
